abstract:
The energy-intensive nature of cryogenic distillation for propylene/propane separation necessitates the development of more sustainable alternatives, such as adsorptive separation using metal–organic frameworks (MOFs). While molecular sieving provides high selectivity by size exclusion, achieving high adsorption capacity remains a significant technical barrier. In this study, we report the rational design of UTSA-400, an oxyfluoride-based ultramicroporous MOF ([Ni(WO$_2$F$_4$)(pyz)$_2$]), engineered to optimize guest binding affinity for propylene while completely excluding propane. 

By substituting traditional pillar ligands with the oxofluorotungstate WO$_2$F$_4^{2–}$ anion, we precisely tuned the pore environment to enhance gas–host interactions. Molecular electrostatic potential mapping and structural analysis reveal that the exposed oxide/fluoride pairs within the one-dimensional channels serve as potent binding sites. Consequently, UTSA-400 exhibits a substantial increase in propylene uptake—exceeding its niobate analog by 544% at 0.1 bar—and achieves a packing density (0.93 g cm$^{–3}$) that surpasses the density of liquid propylene. Experimental in situ infrared spectroscopy and dispersion-corrected density functional theory (DFT-D) calculations confirm that a combination of strong hydrogen bonding and maximized van der Waals contacts underlies this enhanced affinity. 

Dynamic breakthrough experiments demonstrate that UTSA-400 can directly produce polymer-grade (>99.5%) propylene from equimolar mixtures with high productivity. Furthermore, the framework exhibits excellent stability under air, water, and varying pH conditions. This work highlights how atomic-level pore engineering and the regulation of framework dynamics can overcome the traditional capacity–selectivity trade-off in molecular sieving applications.
